info.text: CSIR NET level MCQ questions on Sampling Distributions: chi-square, t, F distributions, order statistics, sample mean and variance distributions, central limit theorem applications, non-central distributions.

questiontext.text: If \(Z_1, \ldots, Z_n \stackrel{iid}{\sim} N(0,1)\), then \(Q = \sum_{i=1}^n Z_i^2 \sim \chi^2_n\). The mean and variance of \(\chi^2_n\) are:

feedback.text: For \(Z\sim N(0,1)\): \(E[Z^2]=1\) and \(\text{Var}(Z^2)=E[Z^4]-(E[Z^2])^2=3-1=2\). By independence: \(E[Q]=n\) and \(\text{Var}(Q)=2n\). Also: MGF of \(\chi^2_n\) is \((1-2t)^{-n/2}\) for \(t<1/2\).
The mean is correct but the variance of \(\chi^2_n\) is \(2n\), not \(n\).
These are the mean and variance of \(\text{Gamma}(n/2, 1)\) where 1 is the rate, i.e., scale \(=2\). With scale \(=2\): mean \(= n/2\times2 = n\). So mean is \(n\), not \(n/2\).
These values are double the correct ones. Mean of \(\chi^2_n\) is \(n\) and variance is \(2n\).

questiontext.text:
If \(Z \sim N(0,1)\) and \(V \sim \chi^2_
u\) are independent, then \(T = \dfrac{Z}{\sqrt{V/
u}} \sim t_
u\). The \(t_
u\) distribution has mean 0 (for \(
u > 1\)) and variance \(\dfrac{
u}{
u-2}\) (for \(
u > 2\)). As \(
u 	o \infty\), the \(t_
u\) distribution converges to:

feedback.text:
As \(
u	o\infty\), \(V/
u \xrightarrow{P} 1\) by the LLN (since \(E[V/
u]=1\) and \(	ext{Var}(V/
u)=2/
u	o0\)). Thus \(T = Z/\sqrt{V/
u} \xrightarrow{d} Z \sim N(0,1)\). Equivalently, the \(t_
u\) variance \(
u/(
u-2)	o 1\) as \(
u	o\infty\).
The Cauchy distribution equals \(t_1\) (1 degree of freedom). As \(\nu\to\infty\), \(t_\nu\to N(0,1)\), not Cauchy.
The chi-square distribution is non-negative and skewed. The \(t\) distribution is symmetric about 0 and converges to \(N(0,1)\).
The limiting distribution of \(t_\nu\) as \(\nu\to\infty\) is \(N(0,1)\), not uniform.

questiontext.text: If \(U \sim \chi^2_m\) and \(V \sim \chi^2_n\) are independent, then \(F = \dfrac{U/m}{V/n} \sim F_{m,n}\). Which relationship between \(F\) and \(t\) distributions is correct?

feedback.text: \(T = Z/\sqrt{V/n}\) where \(Z\sim N(0,1)\). Then \(T^2 = Z^2/(V/n) = \chi^2_1/1 \div \chi^2_n/n \sim F_{1,n}\). Also: \(F_{m,n}\) and \(1/F_{n,m}\) have the same distribution type; if \(F\sim F_{m,n}\) then \(1/F\sim F_{n,m}\).
The degrees of freedom are reversed. \(T^2 = \chi^2_1/\chi^2_n \times n \sim F_{1,n}\), not \(F_{n,1}\).
\(|T|\) does not follow an \(F\) distribution; \(T^2\) (not \(|T|\)) follows \(F_{1,n}\).
\(\sqrt{F_{1,n}} \sim |t_n|\) (half-normal type), not \(t_1\). The \(\sqrt{F_{1,n}}\) is the absolute value of a \(t_n\) variable.

questiontext.text: Let \(X_1, \ldots, X_n \stackrel{iid}{\sim} N(\mu, \sigma^2)\). The sample variance \(S^2 = \dfrac{1}{n-1}\sum_{i=1}^n(X_i - \bar{X})^2\). Then \(\dfrac{(n-1)S^2}{\sigma^2}\) follows:

feedback.text: This follows from the spectral decomposition of the quadratic form: \(\sum(X_i-\bar{X})^2/\sigma^2 = \mathbf{Z}^T(I-J/n)\mathbf{Z}\) where \(\mathbf{Z}\sim N_n(\mathbf{0},I)\). The matrix \(I-J/n\) is idempotent of rank \(n-1\), giving \(\chi^2_{n-1}\). Independence of \(\bar{X}\) and \(S^2\) follows from Cochran's theorem.
The degrees of freedom is \(n-1\) (one df lost for estimating \(\mu\)), not \(n\).
By Cochran's theorem (or direct calculation), \(\bar{X}\) and \(S^2\) are independent for normal populations.
The ratio \((n-1)S^2/\sigma^2\) follows \(\chi^2_{n-1}\), not an \(F\) distribution.

name.text: STAT24-Q05 Order Statistics — Minimum and Maximum

questiontext.text: Let \(X_1, \ldots, X_n \stackrel{iid}{\sim} F\) with pdf \(f\). The pdf of the maximum \(X_{(n)}\) is \(f_{X_{(n)}}(x) = n[F(x)]^{n-1}f(x)\). For \(X_i \stackrel{iid}{\sim} U(0,1)\), the distribution of \(X_{(n)}\) is:

feedback.text: \(\text{Beta}(1,n)\) has pdf \(n(1-x)^{n-1}\), which is the distribution of the minimum \(X_{(1)}\), not the maximum.
This symmetric beta distribution is not the distribution of \(X_{(n)}\).
The maximum of \(n>1\) uniform random variables has a skewed right distribution concentrated near 1, not a uniform distribution.

name.text: STAT24-Q06 Distribution of Sample Mean — CLT

questiontext.text: Let \(X_1, \ldots, X_{100} \stackrel{iid}{\sim}\) distribution with mean 5 and variance 4. By the Central Limit Theorem, the approximate distribution of \(\bar{X} = \dfrac{1}{100}\sum X_i\) is:

feedback.text: By CLT: \(\bar{X}\approx N(\mu, \sigma^2/n) = N(5, 4/100) = N(5, 0.04)\). The standard error is \(\sigma/\sqrt{n} = 2/10 = 0.2\), so \(\bar{X}\approx N(5, 0.04)\) (variance \(=0.04\), SD \(=0.2\)).
This uses the population variance (4), not the sampling variance \(\sigma^2/n = 4/100 = 0.04\).
The variance of \(\bar{X}\) is \(\sigma^2/n = 4/100 = 0.04\), not \(0.4\). Perhaps confused with the standard error \(\sigma/\sqrt{n} = 0.2\).
This is the distribution of \(\sum X_i = 100\bar{X}\), not \(\bar{X}\). Mean of sum is \(100\times5=500\), variance is \(100\times4=400\).

questiontext.text: Cochran's theorem: Let \(\mathbf{X} \sim N_n(\mathbf{0}, \sigma^2 I)\) and \(\mathbf{X}^T\mathbf{X} = \sum_{i=1}^k \mathbf{X}^T A_i \mathbf{X}\) where \(A_i\) are symmetric matrices with \(\sum A_i = I\). Then the quadratic forms \(Q_i = \mathbf{X}^T A_i \mathbf{X}/\sigma^2\) are independently distributed as \(\chi^2_{r_i}\) iff \(\sum r_i = n\), where \(r_i = \text{rank}(A_i)\). In one-way ANOVA with \(k\) groups of size \(n\), the total SS decomposes as \(SS_T = SS_B + SS_W\) where \(SS_T/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2_{kn-1}\). What are the degrees of freedom of \(SS_W/\sigma^2\)?

feedback.text: Total df = \(kn-1\). Between df = \(k-1\). Within df = total \(-\) between \(= (kn-1)-(k-1) = k(n-1)\). By Cochran's theorem, \(SS_B/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2_{k-1}\) and \(SS_W/\sigma^2 \sim \chi^2_{k(n-1)}\) independently.
Note \(kn-k = k(n-1)\), so this is the same as the correct answer. Both expressions are equivalent.
This is the degrees of freedom for \(SS_B\) (between-groups), not \(SS_W\) (within-groups).
Within-group df is \(N-k = kn-k = k(n-1)\), not \(kn-2\).

questiontext.text: If \(Z_i \sim N(\mu_i, 1)\) independently, then \(\sum_{i=1}^n Z_i^2 \sim \chi^2_n(\lambda)\), the non-central chi-square with \(n\) degrees of freedom and non-centrality parameter \(\lambda = \sum_{i=1}^n \mu_i^2\). The mean is \(E[\chi^2_n(\lambda)] =\):

feedback.text: \(E[Z_i^2] = \text{Var}(Z_i) + (E[Z_i])^2 = 1+\mu_i^2\). So \(E[\sum Z_i^2] = n + \sum\mu_i^2 = n+\lambda\). Similarly \(\text{Var}(\chi^2_n(\lambda)) = 2n+4\lambda\). The non-central chi-square reduces to central chi-square when \(\lambda=0\).
This is the mean of the central chi-square. For the non-central case with \(\lambda>0\), the mean is \(n+\lambda\).
The mean includes both the degrees of freedom \(n\) and the non-centrality \(\lambda\): mean \(= n+\lambda\).
The correct mean is \(n+\lambda\). The term \(2\lambda\) appears in the variance formula, not the mean.

questiontext.text: For \(X_1, \ldots, X_n \stackrel{iid}{\sim} U(0,\theta)\), the sample range \(R = X_{(n)} - X_{(1)}\). The joint pdf of \((X_{(1)}, X_{(n)})\) is \(f(u,v) = n(n-1)(v-u)^{n-2}\theta^{-n}\) for \(0 < u < v < \theta\). What is \(E[R]\)?

feedback.text: For \(U(0,\theta)\): \(E[X_{(k)}] = k\theta/(n+1)\). So \(E[R] = E[X_{(n)}] - E[X_{(1)}] = n\theta/(n+1) - \theta/(n+1) = (n-1)\theta/(n+1)\). As \(n\to\infty\), \(E[R]\to\theta\) (the range approaches the full width).
Incorrect; divide by \(n+1\) only (not \(n(n+1)\)). The answer is \((n-1)\theta/(n+1)\).
This would be \(E[X_{(n)}]\) for \(n=1\), or would arise from an incorrect formula. The correct answer involves \((n-1)/(n+1)\).

name.text: STAT24-Q10 Beta Distribution from Order Statistics

questiontext.text: Let \(X_1, \ldots, X_n \stackrel{iid}{\sim} U(0,1)\). The \(k\)-th order statistic \(X_{(k)}\) has the distribution \(\text{Beta}(k, n-k+1)\). Therefore \(E[X_{(k)}] = \dfrac{k}{n+1}\) and \(\text{Var}(X_{(k)}) = \dfrac{k(n-k+1)}{(n+1)^2(n+2)}\). For \(n=4\), the median (average of \(X_{(2)}\) and \(X_{(3)}\)) has mean:

feedback.text: \(E[X_{(2)}] = 2/5\) and \(E[X_{(3)}] = 3/5\). The sample median for \(n=4\) is \((X_{(2)}+X_{(3)})/2\), with mean \(\frac{1}{2}(2/5+3/5) = \frac{1}{2}\times1 = 1/2\). This equals the population median of \(U(0,1)\), confirming the median is unbiased for the population median.
This is \(E[X_{(2)}]\) alone. The sample median is the average of \(X_{(2)}\) and \(X_{(3)}\).
This is \(E[X_{(3)}]\) alone. Average the two middle order statistics to get the median.
This would be \(E[X_{(4)}] = 4/5\), the expected value of the maximum for \(n=4\).

name.text: STAT24-Q11 Delta Method for Sampling Distributions

questiontext.text: If \(\sqrt{n}(\hat{\theta} - \theta) \xrightarrow{d} N(0, \sigma^2)\), then by the delta method, for a differentiable \(g\): \(\sqrt{n}(g(\hat{\theta}) - g(\theta)) \xrightarrow{d} N(0, [g'(\theta)]^2\sigma^2)\). For \(X_1,\ldots,X_n \stackrel{iid}{\sim} \text{Poisson}(\lambda)\), the asymptotic distribution of \(\sqrt{\bar{X}}\) is:

feedback.text: \(\sqrt{n}(\bar{X}-\lambda) \xrightarrow{d} N(0,\lambda)\) (CLT for Poisson). With \(g(x)=\sqrt{x}\), \(g'(\lambda)=1/(2\sqrt{\lambda})\). Delta method: \(\sqrt{n}(\sqrt{\bar{X}}-\sqrt{\lambda})\xrightarrow{d} N(0, \lambda/(4\lambda)) = N(0,1/4)\). This variance-stabilizing transformation makes inference on \(\lambda\) easier.
The asymptotic variance after the delta method is \([g'(\lambda)]^2\sigma^2 = \frac{1}{4\lambda}\cdot\lambda = \frac{1}{4}\), not \(\lambda\).
The asymptotic variance is \(1/4\), not 1. You would divide by 2 to get a standard normal.
The asymptotic variance is \(1/4\), not \(1/(4\lambda)\). The square root transformation stabilizes the variance to \(1/4\) regardless of \(\lambda\).

questiontext.text: For i.i.d. random variables from \(U(0,1)\), the properly normalized maximum satisfies: if \(M_n = \max(X_1,\ldots,X_n)\), then \(n(1-M_n) \xrightarrow{d}\) Exp(1) as \(n\to\infty\). More generally, the Fisher-Tippett-Gnedenko theorem says the limiting distribution of normalized maxima belongs to one of three families. For the exponential distribution, the maximum falls in the domain of attraction of:

feedback.text: For distributions with exponentially decreasing tails (like exponential, normal, gamma), the properly normalized maximum converges to the Gumbel distribution \(\Lambda(x) = e^{-e^{-x}}\). Heavy-tailed distributions (Pareto, Cauchy) attract to Fréchet; bounded distributions (uniform, beta) to Weibull.
The Fréchet distribution attracts distributions with power-law (heavy) tails, such as Pareto and Cauchy. The exponential distribution has light tails.
The Weibull extreme value distribution attracts bounded distributions (like uniform and beta). The exponential distribution is unbounded above.
The normal distribution is the limit of sums (CLT), not of maxima. Maxima from exponential distributions converge to the Gumbel distribution.

questiontext.text: In a survey, \(n=400\) people are asked a yes/no question. If the true proportion is \(p=0.6\), the sample proportion \(\hat{p}\) has an approximate normal distribution by CLT. The probability \(P(|\hat{p} - 0.6| < 0.05)\) is approximately (use \(\Phi(2.04)\approx 0.979\)):

feedback.text: This is \(\Phi(2.04)\), which is one-sided. For a two-sided probability, the answer is \(2\Phi(2.04)-1 = 0.958\).
Incorrect calculation. Standardize: \(0.05/\text{SE} = 0.05/0.0245 \approx 2.04\), giving probability \(\approx 0.958\).
This would be \(P(|\hat{p}-0.6| < \text{SE}) = P(|Z|<1)\approx 0.683\), which uses one standard error, not 0.05.

name.text: STAT24-Q14 Exponential Family and Sufficient Statistics via Sampling

questiontext.text: For \(X_1, \ldots, X_n \stackrel{iid}{\sim} \text{Gamma}(\alpha, \beta)\) with both parameters unknown, the joint sufficient statistic is \((T_1, T_2) = \left(\sum X_i,\, \sum\log X_i\right)\). The Fisher information matrix \(\mathbf{I}(\alpha,\beta)\) has \(I_{11} = \psi'(\alpha)\) where \(\psi'\) is the trigamma function. The entry \(I_{12}\) (off-diagonal) equals:

answer.text: \(-1/\beta\) (the negative reciprocal of the scale parameter)

feedback.text: The log-pdf of Gamma\((\alpha,\beta)\) is \((\alpha-1)\log x - x/\beta - \alpha\log\beta - \log\Gamma(\alpha)\). Cross-derivative: \(\frac{\partial^2}{\partial\alpha\partial\beta}\log f = \frac{1}{\beta}\). So \(I_{12} = -E\!\left[\frac{\partial^2}{\partial\alpha\partial\beta}\log f\right] = -1/\beta\).
The gamma parameters \(\alpha\) and \(\beta\) are not orthogonal; the Fisher information matrix has a non-zero off-diagonal entry \(-1/\beta\).
This is related to \(I_{22}\) (the \((\alpha,\alpha)\) component from the scale parameter). The off-diagonal is \(-1/\beta\).
This equals \(I_{22}\) (the Fisher information for \(\beta\) given \(\alpha\)). The off-diagonal \(I_{12} = -1/\beta\).

questiontext.text: Let \(X_n \sim \text{Binomial}(n, \lambda/n)\) for fixed \(\lambda > 0\). As \(n \to \infty\), the limiting distribution of \(X_n\) is:

feedback.text: The Poisson limit theorem: \(P(X_n=k) = \binom{n}{k}(\lambda/n)^k(1-\lambda/n)^{n-k} \to e^{-\lambda}\lambda^k/k!\) as \(n\to\infty\). This is the "law of rare events" — when \(n\) is large but \(p=\lambda/n\) is small, the Binomial converges to Poisson.
The normal approximation applies when \(n\to\infty\) with fixed \(p\). Here \(p\to0\) as \(n\to\infty\), giving Poisson, not normal.
The CLT requires fixed \(p\) (with \(np\to\infty\)). For \(p=\lambda/n\to0\), the limit is Poisson(\(\lambda\)).
The geometric distribution arises as the limit of negative binomial, not as the Poisson limit theorem.

name.text: STAT24-Q16 Sampling Distribution — Ratio of Chi-Squares

questiontext.text: Let \(X\sim\chi^2_m\) and \(Y\sim\chi^2_n\) be independent. The ratio \(B = \dfrac{X/m}{X/m + Y/n}\) follows a \(\text{Beta}(m/2, n/2)\) distribution. This connection is used because: if \(F\sim F_{m,n}\), then in terms of a Beta-distributed variable:

feedback.text: With \(F = (X/m)/(Y/n)\): \(B = \frac{X/m}{X/m+Y/n} = \frac{F}{F+n/m} = \frac{mF}{mF+n}\). So \(P(F \leq f) = P(B \leq mf/(mf+n))\), allowing \(F\)-distribution quantiles to be computed via the incomplete beta function.
Incorrect transformation. The Beta variable requires the denominator \(mF+n\), not just \(n\).
This formula works only for \(F_{m,m}\) (equal degrees of freedom). In general, the formula involves \(n/m\) in the denominator.
This gives \(1-B\) (the complement), which follows \(\text{Beta}(n/2, m/2)\), not \(\text{Beta}(m/2, n/2)\).

questiontext.text: The Berry-Esseen theorem quantifies the rate of CLT convergence. Let \(X_1, \ldots, X_n\) be i.i.d. with mean 0, variance \(\sigma^2\), and \(E[|X|^3] = \rho < \infty\). Let \(F_n\) be the CDF of \(S_n/(\sigma\sqrt{n})\). Then: \(\sup_x|F_n(x) - \Phi(x)| \leq \dfrac{C\rho}{\sigma^3\sqrt{n}}\) for a universal constant \(C\). The best known \(C\) is approximately 0.4748. This means the CLT approximation error is:

feedback.text: The Berry-Esseen bound is \(O(\rho/(\sigma^3\sqrt{n})) = O(1/\sqrt{n})\). This gives the rate at which the normalized sum converges to normality: the error decreases like \(n^{-1/2}\). Distributions with finite third moment have this convergence rate.
The rate is \(O(1/\sqrt{n})\), not \(O(1/n)\). The \(O(1/n)\) rate would correspond to the Edgeworth expansion correction.
The error is never exactly zero for finite \(n\) (unless the distribution is exactly normal). The rule of thumb "\(n\geq30\)" is just a practical guideline for approximate normality.
Exponential convergence characterizes moment generating function arguments (Cramér's theorem for large deviations), not the CLT uniform convergence rate.

name.text: STAT24-Q18 Multivariate Normal — Marginal and Conditional

questiontext.text: Let \(\begin{pmatrix}X\\Y\end{pmatrix}\sim N_2\!\left(\begin{pmatrix}\mu_X\\\mu_Y\end{pmatrix}, \begin{pmatrix}\sigma_X^2 & \rho\sigma_X\sigma_Y \\ \rho\sigma_X\sigma_Y & \sigma_Y^2\end{pmatrix}\right)\). The conditional distribution of \(Y|X=x\) is:

feedback.text: The conditional mean is \(\mu_{Y|X} = \mu_Y + \Sigma_{YX}\Sigma_{XX}^{-1}(x-\mu_X) = \mu_Y + \rho\sigma_Y/\sigma_X\cdot(x-\mu_X)\) and conditional variance is \(\sigma_{Y|X}^2 = \sigma_Y^2 - \Sigma_{YX}\Sigma_{XX}^{-1}\Sigma_{XY} = \sigma_Y^2(1-\rho^2)\). Note: conditional variance is independent of \(x\).
The regression coefficient should be \(\rho\sigma_Y/\sigma_X\), not just \(\rho\). The units don't match without the ratio \(\sigma_Y/\sigma_X\).
The conditional mean is \(\mu_Y\) only when \(x=\mu_X\). In general, it depends on \(x\) through the regression term.
The conditional variance is \(\sigma_Y^2(1-\rho^2)\), not \(\sigma_Y^2\). Conditioning reduces uncertainty when \(\rho\neq0\).

questiontext.text: Slutsky's theorem: If \(X_n \xrightarrow{d} X\) and \(Y_n \xrightarrow{P} c\) (constant), then \(X_n + Y_n \xrightarrow{d} X + c\) and \(X_n Y_n \xrightarrow{d} cX\). For \(T_n = \dfrac{\sqrt{n}(\bar{X}-\mu)}{S}\) where \(\sqrt{n}(\bar{X}-\mu)/\sigma \xrightarrow{d} N(0,1)\) and \(S \xrightarrow{P} \sigma\), Slutsky's theorem gives \(T_n \xrightarrow{d}\):

feedback.text: \(T_n = \frac{\sqrt{n}(\bar{X}-\mu)}{\sigma} \cdot \frac{\sigma}{S}\). The first factor \(\xrightarrow{d}N(0,1)\), and the second factor \(\sigma/S\xrightarrow{P}1\). By Slutsky's theorem, their product \(\xrightarrow{d}N(0,1)\). This justifies using \(t\)-critical values (approximately \(z\)-values) for large samples.
The exact distribution is \(t_{n-1}\) under normality. But asymptotically (as \(n\to\infty\)), \(T_n\xrightarrow{d}N(0,1)\) by Slutsky's theorem (no normality assumption needed).
The Cauchy distribution is the limit when the denominator has heavy tails or the numerator isn't controlled. Here the denominator \(S\to\sigma\) in probability, giving a normal limit.
After standardizing by \(S\), the limiting variance is 1, not \(\sigma^2\). The \(t\)-statistic is already scaled.

questiontext.text: The empirical CDF is \(F_n(x) = \dfrac{1}{n}\sum_{i=1}^n \mathbf{1}(X_i \leq x)\). The Glivenko-Cantelli theorem states \(\sup_x|F_n(x) - F(x)| \xrightarrow{a.s.} 0\). For fixed \(x\), by the CLT, the asymptotic distribution of \(\sqrt{n}(F_n(x) - F(x))\) is:

feedback.text: \(F_n(x) = \frac{1}{n}\sum\mathbf{1}(X_i\leq x)\) is the mean of i.i.d. Bernoulli\((F(x))\) variables. By CLT: \(\sqrt{n}(F_n(x)-F(x)) \xrightarrow{d} N(0, F(x)(1-F(x)))\). The variance \(F(x)(1-F(x))\) is maximized at \(F(x)=1/2\).
The variance is \(F(x)(1-F(x))\), not 1. The \(N(0,1)\) limit would require dividing by \(\sqrt{F(x)(1-F(x))}\).
After centering by \(F(x)\), the mean is 0. The centering \(\sqrt{n}(F_n(x)-F(x))\) has mean 0.
The CLT gives a normal limit for the rescaled empirical CDF, not a uniform distribution.
